title: SQL Injection in Login via String Concatenation
description: User-supplied email is directly concatenated into a raw SQL query without parameterization, enabling classic SQL injection attacks such as `' OR 1=1--`. An attacker can bypass authentication, enumerate all user accounts, and extract password hashes from the database.

title: SQL Injection in Product Search via String Concatenation
description: The search criteria query parameter is directly concatenated into a raw SQL LIKE query. An attacker can craft search strings to extract arbitrary table data including user credentials and private records via UNION-based or error-based SQL injection.

title: Server-Side Code Injection via eval() in User Profile (SSTI)
description: Usernames matching the pattern `#{...}` have their inner content extracted and executed with `eval()` on the server. An attacker who can set their username to `#{process.mainModule.require('child_process').execSync('id')}` achieves arbitrary remote code execution.

title: XML External Entity (XXE) Injection in File Upload
description: XML files are parsed with `noent: true`, which enables external entity expansion. An attacker can upload a crafted XML file referencing `file:///etc/passwd` or internal network resources to read arbitrary files or perform server-side request forgery.

title: Broken Password Hashing Using MD5
description: Passwords are hashed using MD5, which is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password storage. MD5 produces hashes that can be cracked in seconds using GPU-accelerated rainbow tables or dictionary attacks, exposing all stored user passwords upon a database breach.

title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key Used for JWT Signing
description: The RSA private key used to sign all JWT authentication tokens is hardcoded directly in source code and therefore visible to anyone with repository access. An attacker can use this key to forge valid JWT tokens for any user, including administrators, achieving full authentication bypass.

title: Critically Outdated jsonwebtoken (0.4.0)
description: The application pins `jsonwebtoken` at version 0.4.0; current is 9.x. Versions prior to 8.5.1 contain a critical vulnerability (CVE-2022-23529) allowing algorithm confusion attacks where an attacker can forge tokens by switching to the `none` algorithm. This grants unauthenticated access to any account.

title: Outdated express-jwt (0.1.3) with Known Vulnerabilities
description: express-jwt is pinned at 0.1.3 (current is 8.x). This extremely old version contains multiple CVEs related to improper token validation and algorithm confusion. Combined with the outdated jsonwebtoken library, authentication protections on JWT-guarded routes may be completely bypassed.

title: Outdated sanitize-html (1.4.2) with XSS Bypass
description: sanitize-html is pinned at 1.4.2 (current is 2.x). Early versions contain known XSS bypass vulnerabilities where malformed or nested tags can survive sanitization and execute JavaScript in the browser. Any content processed by this version cannot be trusted as safe HTML.

title: IDOR: UserId Accepted from Request Body in Memory Creation
description: The `UserId` field for creating a memory record is taken directly from the request body rather than from the authenticated session. An attacker can supply any UserId to create records associated with other users, polluting their data or elevating privileges.

title: IDOR: Deluxe Upgrade Performs Operations on Caller-Supplied UserId
description: The deluxe membership upgrade route reads `UserId` from the request body and performs wallet/membership operations without verifying it matches the authenticated session user. An attacker can upgrade arbitrary accounts to deluxe status or drain other users' wallets.

title: Open Redirect via Substring-Based Allowlist Bypass
description: The redirect allowlist check uses `String.includes()` against allowed hostnames, meaning any URL containing an allowed domain as a substring passes validation. An attacker can craft a URL like `https://evil.com/?q=allowed-domain.com` to redirect victims to arbitrary external sites for phishing.

title: Hardcoded HMAC Secret Key in Source Code
description: The HMAC secret `pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj` is hardcoded in the source code. Any party with repository read access can compute valid HMACs for arbitrary payloads, forging integrity-protected data throughout the application.

title: Path Traversal / Zip Slip in File Upload Extraction
description: ZIP entry filenames are resolved with `path.resolve()` but the traversal check uses `includes()` which can be bypassed. A specially crafted ZIP containing entries with paths like `../../server.ts` may overwrite arbitrary files on the server, enabling code execution.

title: Local File Inclusion via Template Layout Parameter
description: The `layout` body parameter is resolved with `path.resolve()` and the only protection is an `includes()` check for a few hardcoded strings. An attacker can pass a path to any server-side file to include it in the rendered template, leaking sensitive configuration or source code.

title: JavaScript Injection via eval() in CAPTCHA Generation
description: A mathematical expression is evaluated directly via `eval()`. If an attacker can influence the expression stored or retrieved for CAPTCHA validation, they can inject arbitrary JavaScript expressions for server-side code execution.

title: Unsafe Deserialization / RCE Risk in B2B Order Processing
description: User-controlled order line data is executed inside a Node.js VM context using `safeEval`. The `notevil` / vm-based sandbox has known escapes and the pattern of running user-supplied code in a VM context does not provide strong isolation, potentially allowing sandbox escape and remote code execution.

title: Stored XSS via bypassSecurityTrustHtml on Product Descriptions
description: Product description HTML from the API response is marked trusted using Angular's `bypassSecurityTrustHtml`, disabling all sanitization. If an attacker injects a malicious script into a product description (via the admin API or SQL injection), it will execute in every user's browser who views search results.

title: Reflected XSS via bypassSecurityTrustHtml on Search Query Parameter
description: The URL search query parameter is passed directly to `bypassSecurityTrustHtml` and rendered in the template. An attacker can craft a URL with `?q=` that executes arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser when they click the link.

title: DOM-Based XSS via innerHTML in Hacking Instructor
description: Hint text is converted from Markdown to HTML via `snarkdown()` and then assigned to `innerHTML` without sanitization. If any hint text contains malicious HTML or if the Markdown parser can be influenced, this results in DOM-based XSS.

title: Directory Listing Enabled on Sensitive Directories
description: The `/ftp`, `/encryptionkeys`, and `/support/logs` paths are served with `serve-index` which enables full directory listing. Attackers can enumerate and download backups, encryption keys, log files, and other sensitive artifacts without authentication.

title: Wildcard CORS Allows Any Origin
description: CORS is configured with default settings (`cors()` with no origin restriction), which allows any website to make credentialed cross-origin requests to the API. Combined with session cookies, this enables cross-site request forgery and unauthorized API access from attacker-controlled pages.

title: Session Token Cookie Missing httpOnly, Secure, and SameSite Flags
description: The JWT token is stored in a cookie set without `httpOnly` (allowing JavaScript access), `secure` (allowing transmission over plain HTTP), or `SameSite` (enabling CSRF). This means XSS vulnerabilities can steal session tokens and CSRF attacks can send authenticated requests.

title: Hardcoded Weak Cookie Parser Secret
description: The cookie-parser middleware is initialized with the hardcoded secret `kekse`. This trivially guessable secret can be used by an attacker to sign forged cookies, bypassing any cookie-integrity checks the application relies upon.

title: YAML Parsing Without Billion-Laughs / Entity Expansion Protection
description: Uploaded YAML files are parsed via `js-yaml` in an old version without configuring safe loading or entity expansion limits. A Billion Laughs-style YAML payload can cause exponential memory growth, leading to denial of service by exhausting server memory.

title: X-XSS-Protection Header Explicitly Disabled
description: The Helmet middleware's `xssFilter()` is commented out, removing the browser-level X-XSS-Protection header. While modern browsers have deprecated this header in favor of CSP, its absence removes a defense-in-depth layer that would otherwise block some reflected XSS attacks in older browsers.

title: XML Content Exposed in Error Messages
description: Up to 400 characters of uploaded XML content is included in error messages passed to the Express error handler. If these errors are surfaced to the client or logged insecurely, this could expose sensitive file content or aid attackers in refining XXE payloads.

title: Outdated js-yaml (^3.14.0) Used in File Parsing
description: js-yaml 3.x is pinned, which uses `safeLoad`-style functions that were renamed and reworked in 4.x after code injection vulnerabilities were discovered. Parsing untrusted YAML with the 3.x `load()` function (as opposed to `safeLoad()`) allows arbitrary JavaScript code execution via YAML deserialization gadgets.
